text: It’s a very popular “classic” textbook on database theory. It’s got some useful information for the uninitiated, but IMO it’s quite dated now and missing all the newest ideas. Unfortunately, I don’t have any newer recommendations, except “Datalog and Logic Databases” for those interested in Datalog.

elements.text: It’s a very popular “classic” textbook on database theory. It’s got some useful information for the uninitiated, but IMO it’s quite dated now and missing all the newest ideas. Unfortunately, I don’t have any newer recommendations, except “Datalog and Logic Databases” for those interested in Datalog.

Very easy gateway, and many very capable people never feel need to go beyond.

text:
Ward Cunningham's "fundamental discovery" was more like a bug fix, or workaround, really. The web wasn't supposed to be read-only. But shipping is better than perfect (worse is better), so we got that. And we saw that it was good (enough). And commercializable.

That's why the old papers of the 1960-80s have so much interesting material for "new" ideas that are desperately needed today. One might have to update the technology they were envisioned in back then, although I'm not even sure about that…

elements.text:

That's why the old papers of the 1960-80s have so much interesting material for "new" ideas that are desperately needed today. One might have to update the technology they were envisioned in back then, although I'm not even sure about that…

text: Love it. I'm always struggling with different ways to surface information from complex graphs, and there are definitely ideas here that I'll want to play with. :slightly_smiling_face:

text:
Yeah, graphs are painful to interact with - which is especially funny considering that most interesting (and thus complex) topics *are* graphs. Indeed, I'm becoming increasingly convinced that all content is a view of some graph, and we're just stuck interacting with whatever projection was chosen (e.g. text).

Anything in particular you liked, were confused about or can think of <@U013ZLJARC7> ?

text:
Being implemented in Java and being able to run Java gives Java on Truffle a very interesting property: it can run itself. Indeed, Java on Truffle is a metacircular VM, it can run itself several levels deep (albeit slower and slower every time)

<https://medium.com/graalvm/java-on-truffle-going-fully-metacircular-215531e3f840>

attachments.title: Java on Truffle — Going Fully Metacircular
attachments.text: Up until now, GraalVM has offered two ways to run Java programs: using the Java HotSpot VM with the GraalVM JIT (just-in-time) compiler…
attachments.fallback: Medium: Java on Truffle — Going Fully Metacircular

text:
Hi all! I've been leeching here for a bit, but never properly introduced myself :face_with_hand_over_mouth:..

I'm Yousef, from Amsterdam, and lifelong programmer + startup. If there are any outdoor enthusiast here you might be familiar with Relive, a startup I cofounded and most successful project so far (app with 10M+ users).

Like many of you here I've always been interested in programming environments, probably triggered by Bret Victor  a long time ago :male_mage:.

Currently, I'm experimenting with a Reactive Live Programming environment, with support for TypeScript, React, NPM / esm modules. I hope to make this an open source project and to share this with you soon.

For now, I've recorded a first demo @ <https://www.loom.com/share/9f9f91a10348478f9bb0300220452b10>. Would be really cool to hear your feedback, and looking forward to being an active member of this community!

attachments.title: TypeScriptRepl
attachments.video_html: <iframe src="https://www.loom.com/embed/9f9f91a10348478f9bb0300220452b10?autoplay=1" frameborder="0" width="1280" height="960" webkitallowfullscreen mozallowfullscreen allowfullscreen></iframe>

text:
Looks really great and polished.

What would you say the main distinguishing features are vs. Jupyter and Observable?

elements.text:
Looks really great and polished.

What would you say the main distinguishing features are vs. Jupyter and Observable?

text:
Thanks!! Was a little nervous to share a first video ;)

Observable is definitely a big inspiration. I started with the project to see what observable with first-class typescript+react support could look like. 

I’m also doing some experiments to go beyond the traditional “notebook” use cases. Hope to share more on that later.


For now, the main differences are:
• evaluate as you type
• Built on top of Monaco (VS Code).
• Import any module from npm (including types), uses Skypack
• First-class typescript / javascript support (observable introduces some new keywords, id like to prevent that)
• React / tsx support
• change language of cells (now markdown + typescript are supported)
• The “reactive part” is built on MobX

elements.text: Thanks!! Was a little nervous to share a first video ;)

Observable is definitely a big inspiration. I started with the project to see what observable with first-class typescript+react support could look like. 

I’m also doing some experiments to go beyond the traditional “notebook” use cases. Hope to share more on that later.


For now, the main differences are:
evaluate as you type
Built on top of Monaco (VS Code).
Import any module from npm (including types), uses Skypack
First-class typescript / javascript support (observable introduces some new keywords, id like to prevent that)
React / tsx support
change language of cells (now markdown + typescript are supported)
The “reactive part” is built on MobX
